exam_notes_and_clearance.notes.answer:
Ethan is an 18-year-old male high school senior presenting for an annual preparticipation physical examination for competitive varsity soccer and track (400m and 800m). He reports training year-round with his club soccer team and school programs, averaging 5–6 days per week of activity including practices, games, and strength conditioning.

Medical history (per athlete and parent interview during exam):
- No history of syncope, presyncope, or chest pain with exertion. Denies palpitations, unexplained shortness of breath, or exercise intolerance beyond expected fatigue from intensive training.
- No known congenital heart disease, arrhythmias, or structural cardiac abnormalities. No prior cardiology evaluations or echocardiograms indicated.
- Family history negative for sudden cardiac death, hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, long QT syndrome, Marfan syndrome, or unexplained drowning or single-vehicle accidents in first-degree relatives under age 50.
- No history of asthma or exercise-induced bronchospasm. No history of wheezing, nighttime cough, or use of inhalers.
- No history of concussion, loss of consciousness, or significant head injury. Has had minor heading of the ball in soccer as expected, with no prolonged symptoms afterward.
- Musculoskeletal history notable only for a mild right ankle sprain at age 16 during a soccer match, treated conservatively with rest, ice, compression, elevation, and a brief course of physical therapy. Full range of motion and strength restored; no instability or recurrent sprains reported since.
- No prior fractures, dislocations, or surgeries. No history of overuse injuries such as stress fractures, Osgood-Schlatter disease, or patellofemoral pain syndrome.
- No history of heat illness, rhabdomyolysis, or significant dehydration. Athlete and parent both report good hydration habits, especially during tournaments and summer training.
- No chronic medical conditions (e.g., diabetes, seizure disorders, sickle cell disease). No known allergies to medications, foods, or environmental exposures.
- Current medications: none, including no performance-enhancing drugs, supplements, or regular over-the-counter medication use beyond occasional ibuprofen for post-game soreness.
- Sleep: averages 7–8 hours per night on school nights, 8–9 hours on weekends. Reports feeling generally well-rested.
- Nutrition: reports eating three main meals and 1–2 snacks daily, with emphasis on lean proteins (chicken, fish), complex carbohydrates (rice, pasta, whole grains), fruits, vegetables, and adequate water intake. Drinks occasional sports drinks during games and practices. Minimal intake of sugary sodas and energy drinks; denies use of pre-workout stimulants.
- Substance use: denies tobacco, vaping, alcohol, or recreational drug use. Denies use of anabolic steroids or other performance-enhancing substances.
- Mental health: denies persistent sadness, anhedonia, or anxiety interfering with school or sports. Reports occasional pre-game nervousness that resolves once play begins. No history of counseling, psychiatric diagnoses, or psychotropic medications. Denies self-harm or suicidal ideation.
- Immunizations: reported up to date per parent, including Tdap booster and COVID-19 vaccination series.

Physical exam summary: well-developed, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Normal vital signs for age and conditioning status. Cardiovascular exam normal with regular rate and rhythm, normal S1/S2, no murmurs at rest or with dynamic maneuvers (standing, supine, Valsalva, or after brief exertion). No Marfanoid habitus (arm span not significantly greater than height, no pectus deformity, negative wrist and thumb signs). Lungs clear to auscultation bilaterally with good air movement and no wheezes, rales, or rhonchi. Abdomen soft, non-tender, no organomegaly or masses. Skin examination reveals no concerning lesions, rashes, or evidence of MRSA or impetigo. No hernias appreciated. Lymph nodes non-tender and not enlarged.

Musculoskeletal exam: full active and passive range of motion in all major joints (cervical spine, shoulders, elbows, wrists, hips, knees, ankles) with symmetric strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities. No joint swelling, effusion, or deformity. Special tests for knee (Lachman, anterior/posterior drawer, varus/valgus stress) negative bilaterally. Ankle stability tests (anterior drawer, talar tilt) normal bilaterally, including previously sprained right ankle. Gait normal, able to perform heel and toe walking, single-leg squat, and duck walk without pain or instability.

Overall impression: healthy, well-conditioned adolescent athlete with no current medical or orthopedic issues that would limit full participation in high-intensity competitive sports. No additional testing or specialty referrals indicated at this time. Athlete and parent counseled on maintaining hydration, gradual progression of training intensity at the start of each season, appropriate use of rest days, stretching and strengthening (particularly core and lower extremity) to prevent overuse injuries, and prompt reporting of concerning symptoms such as chest pain, unexplained shortness of breath, or syncope during exercise.

exam_notes_and_clearance.recommendations.answer:
1. Cleared for full participation in varsity soccer and track without restriction.
2. Recommended continuation of current conditioning program with emphasis on:
   - Lower extremity strengthening (hips, glutes, hamstrings, calves) to support knee and ankle stability.
   - Dynamic warm-up prior to practices and games (e.g., light jog, high knees, butt kicks, lunges, leg swings) and static stretching afterward to reduce injury risk.
3. Encourage ongoing attention to hydration: begin hydrating at least 1–2 hours before practice, continue with water or electrolyte solution during intense or prolonged sessions, and rehydrate afterward based on weight loss from sweat.
4. Advise use of appropriate, well-fitted cleats and replacement of worn footwear to maintain proper support, especially given prior right ankle sprain history.
5. Counsel on avoiding over-the-counter NSAID overuse; use only as needed for short-term soreness and not as a pre-emptive measure before every practice or game.
6. Instruct athlete and parent to monitor for any new symptoms, particularly chest pain, unexplained shortness of breath, palpitations, dizziness, or fainting with exertion. If such symptoms occur, advised to stop activity immediately and seek prompt medical evaluation.
7. Recommend maintaining current healthy nutrition and sleep habits, with goal of at least 8 hours of sleep per night during the competitive season.
8. Advised annual preparticipation physical prior to each new school sports year or sooner if any significant injury or new medical condition arises.

physician_information.address.answer: Evergreen Sports Medicine Clinic, 7428 Brookside Ave, Seattle, WA 98115
